title: Constants should come first in equality tests
description: Constants should come first in equality testsAnalyze your codeconsistency-conventionalmaintainabilityCode SmellWhy is this an issue?The result of the comparison is the same, regardless of whether the constant is on the left or right-hand side. But following this convention will help pinpoint the occasional error where=(assignment) is substituted for==(comparison).If the constant is on the right-hand side of the expression in such cases, the code will still compile and run - just not as expected. If the constant is on the left-hand side, the error will be caught at the first attempt to compile.Noncompliant code exampleif ( var == constant ) if ( pointer == NULL )Compliant solutionif ( constant == var ) if ( NULL == pointer )Available I
recommendation: Remove the problematic code and refactor as needed.

noncompliant: if (var == constant) { ... }  // Noncompliant: constant on right side
if (pointer == NULL) { ... }  // Noncompliant: NULL on right side
compliant: if (constant == var) { ... }  // Compliant: constant on left side
if (NULL == pointer) { ... }  // Compliant: NULL on left side

checks.message: Constants should come first in equality tests. Remove the problematic code and refactor as needed.
